Document [6] (Title: Spinal Cord Subacute Combined Degeneration -- Introduction) SCD can be caused by the use and misuse of nitrous oxide. [2] It can also be caused by copper deficiency resulting from excessive zinc consumption.[3] These are less common yet important causes to recognize in clinical practice. Unexplained anemia, coupled with neurological symptoms, should raise suspicions of SCD, and an evaluation investigating vitamin B12 or, less commonly, copper deficiency in susceptible populations should be initiated.
Document [7] (Title: Spinal Cord Subacute Combined Degeneration -- Etiology) SCD can be caused by a functional deficit of vitamin B12 secondary to repeated use of nitrous oxide. Nitrous oxide inactivates vitamin B12 by oxidizing the cobalt in the vitamin B12 molecule. The functionally deficient vitamin B12 causes impaired methionine synthase activity, which leads to impaired myelin maintenance and axonal degeneration.
Document [8] (Title: Spinal Cord Subacute Combined Degeneration -- Pathophysiology) Nitrous oxide inactivates vitamin B12 by oxidation of the cobalt ion in cobalamin and makes the vitamin B12 functionally inactive. As a consequence, the methionine synthase activity is reduced. Reduced methionine results in myelin maintenance failure and secondary axonal degeneration in SCD. [19]

Document [10] (Title: Spinal Cord Subacute Combined Degeneration -- Evaluation) The initial evaluation of a patient with subacute neurological symptoms and anemia should include a complete blood count, peripheral smear, and serum vitamin B12 and folate levels. Specific indices, especially mean corpuscular volume (MCV), will reveal a macrocytic anemia (MCV >100 fL). [22] Macrocytosis may occur without anemia. However, an MCV >115 fL is considered more specific for B12 deficiency and helps differentiate it from other causes of macrocytosis. [23] Hypersegmented neutrophils and macro-ovalocytes are present in the peripheral smear. A typical differential for megaloblastic anemia is folate deficiency; however, neurological findings are rare in folate deficiency. While homocysteine can be elevated in folate and B12 deficiency, methylmalonic acid(MMA) is elevated only in B12 deficiency. Therefore, an elevation of both MMA and homocysteine suggests B12 deficiency. Elevation of homocysteine with normal MMA indicates folate deficiency. An elevated MMA and elevated homocysteine levels would rule out a diagnosis of folate deficiency. Methylmalonic acid(MMA) is considered a more accurate marker of deficiency than homocysteine or B12 levels. Nitrous oxide use must be seriously considered if the vitamin B12 level is normal, yet the MMA is high. [19] If the vitamin B12 and MMA levels are normal and the patient presents with signs and symptoms of SCD, it is important to check the serum levels of copper, ceruloplasmin, and zinc to rule out copper deficiency myelopathy. [8]

Document [17] (Title: Initial Antepartum Care -- Issues of Concern -- Dietary recommendations) Increased amounts of these micronutrients are necessary to support fetal growth and development and replace subsequent maternal deficiencies secondary to gestational demands. For instance, iron deficiency resulting in first-trimester anemia is associated with preterm delivery and low birth weight, and choline supplementation may improve fetal neurodevelopment, particularly in women with a history of alcohol abuse. Moreover, several studies have demonstrated that certain micronutrients may help prevent preeclampsia. [48] Therefore, clinicians should encourage patients to consume foods that contain these essential nutrients, including fortified cereals, pasta, legumes, bread, cheese, yogurt, salmon, and eggs, ideally before conception. [42] Folic acid supplementation of 400 μg daily is recommended in all pregnant women to prevent neural tube defects; in patients with a high risk for neural tube defects, including those with a family history of neural tube defects, taking antiepileptic drugs, with pregestational diabetes, or with obesity, the optimal dosage for folic acid supplementation is less defined, ranging from 1,000 to 4,000 μg daily. [49] In patients with anemia, additional iron supplementation may be prescribed empirically. Several daily oral iron supplements are available, including ferrous sulfate and ferrous gluconate. Intravenous formulations may be used in select patients (eg, intolerant of oral therapy or severe anemia). [31] Vegetarian and vegan pregnant patients should be advised to supplement nutrients (eg, vitamins B 12 and D) primarily found in animal products. [50] Clinicians should also advise patients not to follow any weight-loss diet or fad diet that restricts the consumption of critical nutrients during pregnancy. [48]
Document [18] (Title: Biotin Deficiency -- Deterrence and Patient Education -- Emerging Therapeutic Roles of Biotin) Multiple sclerosis: Clinical data show that patients with multiple sclerosis, when treated with daily biotin doses of up to 300 mg, respond positively, with a reversal in disease progression and a reduction in chronic disability. The likely mechanism is due to increased myelin production leading to increased axonal remyelination. Biotin may also increase energy production and decrease axonal hypoxia in multiple sclerosis. [24] [24] Though not yet a standard of care, biotin may represent a novel adjunctive therapy in other neurodegenerative disorders and is being explored in ongoing trials. [25] [26]

Document [30] (Title: Spinal Cord Subacute Combined Degeneration -- Pearls and Other Issues) Spinal cord SCD is a clinical syndrome characterized by involvement and degeneration of the dorsal and lateral columns (white matter tracts due to demyelination). These patients present with impairment of dorsal column sensation, sensory ataxia, and Rombergism, associated with upper motor neuron signs of spasticity and positive Babinski signs. The most typical cause is vitamin B12 deficiency due to impaired intake or absorption. The treatment response is excellent. Recognizing nitrous oxide (Whippits) misuse is important, especially in young patients. Copper deficiency myelopathy due to impaired copper absorption or excessive zinc intake must be seriously considered if the patient's serum B12 and methylmalonic acid levels are normal.

Document [3] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2039) Iron in other combinations is classified as an iron antianemic preparation. Iron antianemic preparations are therapeutic agents used to replenish iron stores and treat iron‑deficiency anemia. Formulations described as “iron in other combinations” contain iron together with additional compounds or nutrients, enhancing absorption or providing complementary benefits while still serving the primary purpose of correcting iron deficiency. Recognizing these combination products as a subset of iron antianemic preparations helps clinicians select appropriate therapy for patients with anemia, especially when co‑existing deficiencies or specific tolerability issues are present.

Folic acid, a key component of these products, supports normal hematopoiesis, enhances iron absorption, and prevents nutritional deficiencies that can cause anemia; its deficiency is directly associated with macrocytic (megaloblastic) anemia. Document [18] (Title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 948) Folic acid (kyselina listová) is a common active ingredient in a wide range of multivitamin and iron–containing supplements. It is listed as an ingredient in “Multivitamins w/ Iron Oral Capsules,” “VITAMIN B COMPLEX W/C & IRON CAP,” “VITAMIN B COMPLEX W/C,IRON,ZINC TAB,” and “VITAMIN B/FERRIC PYROPHOSPHATE 100MG/FA 0.25MG LIQUID.” Complex formulations such as the oral capsule containing calcium ascorbate 60 mg, calcium threonate 0.8 mg, ferrous asparto‑glycinate 50 mg, folic acid 1 mg, polysaccharide iron complex 100 mg, succinic acid 50 mg, and vitamin B12 0.025 mg also include folic acid, as does the capsule with folic acid 1 mg, polysaccharide iron complex 150 mg, and vitamin B12 0.025 mg. Additional branded products that incorporate folic acid together with iron include “multivitamin with iron Multiple Vitamins with Folic Acid and Iron oral tablet,” “multivitamin with iron Vitamin B Complex with C, Folic Acid and Iron oral capsule,” “multivitamin with iron Vitamin B Complex with C, Folic Acid and Iron oral tablet,” “multivitamin with iron Vitamin B Complex with C, Folic Acid, Iron and Docusate oral tablet,” “multivitamin with iron Vitamin B12 with C, Folic Acid and Iron oral tablet,” “multivitamin with iron Vitamin B12 with Folic Acid and Iron oral capsule,” and “multivitamin with iron Vitamin C with Folic Acid and Iron oral tablet, extended release.” The presence of folic acid in these preparations supports its role in preventing and treating folate‑deficiency macrocytic anemia (فقر دم ضَخم الأرومات), a condition characterized by abnormally large red blood cells that improves with adequate folic acid supplementation. Consequently, clinicians often prescribe or recommend these folic‑acid‑containing multivitamin‑iron products to address both nutritional deficiencies and the hematologic consequences of insufficient folate.
Document [19] (Title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 4 -- Community 2793) Iron trivalent, oral antianemic preparations represent a therapeutic class of iron‑based agents used to treat iron‑deficiency anemia, and this class includes specific compounds such as ferric hydroxide and sodium ironedetate. Both ferric hydroxide and sodium ironedetate are members of the iron trivalent, oral antianemic preparations group, providing clinicians with oral options to replenish deficient iron stores in patients. Understanding that these individual agents belong to the same antianemic class helps guide appropriate selection and dosing in patient care.
Document [20] (Title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1798) Folic acid (kyselina listová) is a key B‑vitamin that appears as an active ingredient in a wide range of multivitamin and iron‑containing supplements, underscoring its importance for hematologic health and overall nutrition. The bariatric chewable tablet “BARIATRIC MULTIVITAMINS W/IRON TAB, CHEWABLE” lists folic acid among its components, as do the complex oral tablets marketed as “BETA CAROTENE 2850 [iU] / ASCORBIC ACID 120 mg / CHOLECALCIFEROL 840 [iU] / .ALPHA.-TOCOPHEROL ACETATE, DL‑3 mg / THIAMINE MONONITRATE 1.8 mg / RIBOFLAVIN 4 mg / NIACINAMIDE 20 mg / PYRIDOXINE HYDROCHLORIDE 50 mg / FOLIC ACID 1 mg / CYANOCOBALAMIN .012 mg / CALCIUM CARBONATE 219 mg / SODIUM FEREDETATE 25.65 mg / MAGNESIUM OXIDE 25 mg / ZINC OXIDE 25 mg / CUPRIC OXIDE 2 mg / IRON SUCROSE 1.35 mg / IODINE .223 mg ORAL TABLET [Bal‑Care DHA Essential]” and its counterpart without “Essential”. The liquid formulation “VITAMIN B/FERRIC PYROPHOSPHATE 100MG/FA 0.25MG LIQUID” and the capsule “folic acid 1 MG / polysaccharide iron complex 150 MG / vitamin B12 0.025 MG Oral Capsule” also contain folic acid. In addition, several generic multivitamin products that combine iron with other nutrients—namely “Multiple Vitamins with Folic Acid and Iron oral tablet,” “Vitamin B Complex with C, Folic Acid and Iron oral capsule,” “Vitamin B Complex with C, Folic Acid and Iron oral tablet,” and “Vitamin B Complex with C, Folic Acid, Iron and Docusate oral tablet”—list folic acid as an ingredient. Clinically, folic acid is directly linked to the prevention and treatment of macrocytic (megaloblastic) anemia, a condition reflected in the relationship “kyselina listová – فقر دم ضَخم الأرومات.” Consequently, inclusion of folic acid in these diverse supplement formulations supports red‑cell maturation, mitigates anemia risk, and provides essential nutritional support, particularly for patients requiring iron supplementation, those undergoing bariatric surgery, or individuals with increased vitamin B needs.
Document [21] (Title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 817) IRON/MULTIVITAMINS is a comprehensive multivitamin formulation that combines iron with a broad spectrum of micronutrients and calcium to support daily nutritional needs. The product is offered in multi‑day regimens that provide extra iron alongside calcium, and it is available in several oral dosage forms, including tablets, capsules, and liquid preparations, some of which are extended‑release. Its iron component is supplied through multiple compounds such as polysaccharide iron complex, ferrous asparto‑glycinate, and ferric pyrophosphate, ensuring varied bioavailability. Calcium is included as calcium ascorbate and calcium threonate, contributing to bone health. The formulation also contains folic acid, vitamin B12, and a full vitamin B complex, as well as vitamin C, vitamin A, vitamin D, and essential fatty acids, providing antioxidant support, immune function, and metabolic benefits. By integrating these ingredients, IRON/MULTIVITAMINS aims to address iron deficiency, support hematologic health, and deliver additional vitamins and minerals essential for overall patient well‑being.
Document [22] (Title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 953) ANTIANEMIC PREPARATIONS represent a broad therapeutic class that includes iron antianemic preparations and formulations containing folic acid and vitamin B12. Iron antianemic preparations are a specific subgroup of antianemic preparations, and within this subgroup the category “Iron in other combinations” is defined as a type of iron antianemic preparation. The product identified as FOLIC ACID/IRON/MULTIVITAMS is classified as a member of “Iron in other combinations,” thereby placing it within both the iron antianemic and the overall antianemic preparation categories. FOLIC ACID/IRON/MULTIVITAMS contains a complex mixture of active ingredients. Document [24] (Title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1270) Iron bivalent, oral antianemic preparations are a specific type of iron antianemic preparations and are classified as members of the askorbát železnatý (iron‑ascorbate) group. As oral formulations that provide bivalent iron, they are used to treat iron‑deficiency anemia, aligning them with the broader class of iron antianemic agents while being distinguished by their inclusion in the iron‑ascorbate category.
Document [25] (Title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1455) IRON/MULTIVITAMINS represents a group of oral iron‑containing multivitamin products that are formulated with a variety of micronutrients and mineral sources to support nutritional needs. The category includes formulations such as “MULTIVITAMINS W/IRON DROPS, ORAL” and “VITAMIN B COMPLEX W/C & IRON TAB,” each of which contains iron combined with other vitamins and minerals. Specific ingredient profiles described for IRON/MULTIVATAMS include calcium ascorbate 60 mg, calcium threonate 0.8 mg, ferrous asparto glycinate 50 mg, folic acid 1 mg, polysaccharide iron complex 100 mg (or 150 mg in an alternative formulation), succinic acid 50 mg, and vitamin B12 0.025 mg, all delivered in oral capsules. Additional product variants are described as “multivitamin with iron Multiple Vitamins with Folic Acid and Iron oral tablet,” “multivitamin with iron Vitamin B Complex with C, Folic Acid and Iron oral capsule,” “multivitamin with iron Vitamin B Complex with C, Folic Acid and Iron oral tablet,” “multivitamin with iron Vitamin B12 with C, Folic Acid and Iron oral capsule,” “multivitamin with iron Vitamin B12 with C, Folic Acid and Iron oral tablet,” and “multivitamin with iron Vitamin A, D and C with Iron oral liquid.” These variants illustrate that the products are available in capsule, tablet, and liquid forms and may contain different combinations of vitamin A, C, D, B‑complex, folic acid, and iron. The relationship “Multi‑Day with Calcium and Extra Iron” is identified as a narrower formulation within this group, indicating a specific multi‑day dosing regimen that emphasizes additional calcium alongside iron. Collectively, these IRON/MULTIVITAMINS products provide integrated supplementation of iron with calcium, folic acid, vitamin B12, vitamin C, and other essential vitamins, offering flexible dosing options to address iron deficiency and support overall micronutrient status.

Document [29] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1256) FERROUS AMINO ACID CHELATE is a pharmaceutical preparation that combines iron with folic acid and is classified as a member of the iron‑in‑combination‑with‑folic‑acid group, reflecting its role in providing both elemental iron and folate for the treatment of iron deficiency and support of folate-dependent metabolic processes.
